planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; ivory sedge is native to rocky, calcareous slopes and performs surprisingly well in dry shade under trees where few other ornamentals survive.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to rocky soil in partial to deep shade; ivory sedge is one of the finest native sedges for extremely challenging shade conditions — it grows naturally on shaded limestone bluffs and rocky slopes where deep shade and dry, rocky soils combine to exclude nearly all other plants. The very fine, delicate, bright-green, almost thread-like leaves form soft, airy tufts 6–12 inches tall that sway gracefully with the slightest air movement, creating a cloud-like texture unlike the bolder carex species. It is one of very few ornamental plants that genuinely tolerates deep dry shade under dense, shallow-rooted trees — a notoriously difficult garden situation. Unlike most sedges, it performs well in neutral to slightly alkaline soils and requires sharp drainage. The delicate texture makes it an outstanding companion to bold-leafed hostas and ferns in the shade border. Semi-evergreen to evergreen in most growing zones.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Variegata spreads slowly by rhizome to form a weed-suppressing groundcover mat and is best left undivided for 3–4 years to develop its most effective coverage.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Variegata creeping broadleaf sedge forms a slowly spreading carpet of broad, strap-like leaves cleanly edged with creamy-white margins that appear luminous in shaded positions. The leaves are wider than most sedges — 1/2 to 3/4 inch — giving the plant a decidedly bolder texture than fine-leafed species. The white-margined foliage brightens shaded areas through summer, combining well with dark-leafed hostas, astilbes, and ferns. The plant emerges late in spring and looks freshest from spring through early summer; in hot, humid climates some leaf tips may brown by late summer, which can be trimmed. Semi-evergreen in zone 7–9; may die back in zone 5–6 winters but re-emerges vigorously. Divide when clumps become crowded, about every 4 years, replanting vigorous outer sections and discarding the woody center.

common_name: Creek Sedge (Narrow-Leaf Sedge, Eastern Gray Sedge)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; creek sedge is highly adaptable to a wide range of soil moisture conditions, from consistently moist woodland soils to seasonally wet streambanks.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; creek sedge is a graceful native sedge of the southeastern and central US, forming upright to arching tufts of narrow, dark-green, somewhat glaucous leaves useful for naturalized streambanks, rain gardens, shaded borders, and moist-shade situations under trees. It is one of the most ecologically important native sedges of the Southeast, providing ground-level structure and habitat in woodland understories and riparian edges throughout the region. The species is widely distributed from New England to Florida and westward to the central plains, demonstrating its adaptability across a broad range of climates. It tolerates both consistently moist garden soils and temporary flooding. In zone 9, it performs as an effective semi-evergreen groundcover in partially shaded areas receiving moderate moisture. An outstanding choice for sustainable landscape plantings, rain garden edges, and woodland restoration.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Japanese anemone establishes slowly in year one but becomes progressively more vigorous, eventually spreading by stolons to form large colonies. Do not disturb established plants.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Japanese anemone is one of the most valuable shade perennials for late-season interest, producing graceful 2–4 foot stems of single or semi-double flowers in white or soft pink in late summer and fall when most shade plants have finished blooming. The large, poppy-like flowers — 2–3 inches across — carry prominent yellow stamens that glow against the petal color. The deeply lobed, dark-green, fig-like basal leaves provide attractive texture through summer before the flower stems emerge. Popular cultivars include 'Honorine Jobert' (pure white, single, the most widely grown), 'September Charm' (single pink), 'Max Vogel' (semi-double pink), and 'Whirlwind' (semi-double white). Japanese anemones are slow to establish in their first year but become progressively robust, eventually spreading by underground stolons to form dramatic colonies. They are largely deer-resistant and an outstanding choice for filling the autumn shade garden with graceful color.

planting.note: Plant small divisions or cuttings in spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic, very well-drained, rocky or gravelly soil in partial to full shade; wild stonecrop establishes easily from stem cuttings pressed into moist soil and spreads to form dense mats in appropriate conditions.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, slightly acidic, well-drained, rocky or gravelly soil in partial shade; wild stonecrop is the only native Sedum of eastern North America adapted to shade — a low, mat-forming succulent producing whorls of three small, fleshy, bright-green rounded leaves on trailing stems that root where they contact moist soil. In spring, upright flowering stems rise 3–6 inches to produce clusters of small, star-shaped white flowers attractive to early pollinators. It is an outstanding native groundcover for shaded rocky slopes, woodland paths, and difficult spots where soil is dry, well-drained, and in partial shade — conditions where most shade plants struggle. Unlike other stonecrops, which demand full sun, wild stonecrop genuinely thrives in shade and performs poorly in direct sun. It spreads at a moderate rate to form solid mats that suppress weeds and stabilize slopes. Deer usually ignore it. An underused but excellent native for naturalized woodland groundcover plantings.

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; soapwort gentian is demanding in its moisture requirements and is best planted at the margin of moist woodland areas or along stream banks.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; soapwort gentian is a late-blooming native perennial producing striking, intensely deep-blue, bottle-shaped flowers in October and November — among the latest blooming native wildflowers and a vital nectar source for bumblebees in the fall garden. The flowers remain tightly closed at the top — the bumblebee must force its way inside to access the nectar, making soapwort gentian one of the most specialized pollinator plants for late-season bees. The upright stems carry pairs of opposite leaves, topped by clusters of 1–3 brilliant blue blooms. Consistent moisture is non-negotiable — this plant grows naturally at forest edges, moist meadows, and streambanks in the eastern US and will quickly decline if allowed to dry out. The extraordinary blue flower color in October distinguishes it from any other native plant in the fall shade garden.

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; black cohosh is a native woodland plant that establishes slowly but eventually forms large, permanent clumps that should not be moved once established.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; black cohosh is one of the most dramatic native shade perennials of eastern North America — a bold woodland plant with large, deeply divided, compound leaves rising from thick rhizomes to form a substantial mound 3–4 feet wide, topped in summer by extraordinary wand-like flower spikes reaching 4–7 feet. The white flowers on tall bottlebrush-like racemes have a subtle, slightly musky fragrance and are followed by small, round green seed capsules in fall. The rhizomes are widely used in traditional medicine and herbal supplements for menopausal symptoms; over-collection from wild populations makes garden cultivation an important conservation measure. In the garden it is outstanding under tall trees where its white summer flowers illuminate the dark understory. Purple-leafed cultivars such as 'Hillside Black Beauty' and 'Brunette' add dramatic dark foliage interest in addition to the white summer flowers.

planting.note: Plant dormant rhizome divisions in fall or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; bellwort is a slow-growing native woodland perennial that performs best left undisturbed once established.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; large-flowered bellwort is a charming native woodland perennial producing elegant arching stems with perfoliate leaves — the stem appears to pierce through the center of each leaf — and pendant, pale yellow, twisted bell-shaped flowers in mid-spring resembling small, nodding lilies. Plants emerge early and bloom for 2–4 weeks before the woodland canopy leafs out and deep shade closes in. After flowering, stems elongate and the leaves expand to 3–4 inches wide, providing attractive texture through summer. Bellwort slowly spreads by rhizome to form substantial colonies in ideal conditions of consistently moist, acidic, deep, humus-rich soil in dappled shade. It combines naturally with trillium, Virginia bluebells, bleeding heart, and other spring woodland plants. The wide native range (Quebec to South Carolina and westward to Kansas) indicates broad adaptability, and it grows reliably in zone 9 with adequate moisture.
